title: Quiz: Merkle Trees for Signatures
description: Test your understanding of Merkle tree construction and signature schemes

questions.question: In a Merkle tree of height h, how many signatures can be generated?
questions.explanation: A Merkle tree of height h has 2^h leaves. Each leaf corresponds to one OTS key pair, so 2^h signatures can be generated before the key is exhausted.

questions.question: What is the authentication path in a Merkle signature?

options.text: The path from root to leaf
The sibling nodes from leaf to root
All nodes at the same level as the leaf

questions.explanation: The authentication path contains the h sibling nodes from the leaf to the root. This allows a verifier to recompute the root hash starting from the leaf.

questions.question: Why is state management critical in Merkle signature schemes?

options.text: To prevent OTS key reuse which would break security

questions.explanation: Each OTS key can only be used once. If the same leaf is used twice, an attacker can combine the two signatures to forge new signatures. State management ensures each leaf is used exactly once.

questions.question: For a Merkle tree with height 20, how many hash values are in the authentication path?
questions.explanation: The authentication path contains one sibling hash for each level of the tree. With height h=20, there are 20 hashes in the authentication path.

questions.question: What is the public key in a Merkle signature scheme?
questions.explanation: The public key is just the Merkle root hash - a single hash value that commits to all 2^h OTS public keys. This is what makes the scheme practical.

questions.question: What happens if a signer restores from a backup after signing messages?

options.text: Risk of OTS key reuse, compromising security

questions.explanation: Restoring from backup reverts the state to an older point. New signatures might reuse OTS keys that were used after the backup, allowing forgery attacks.

questions.question: How does the verifier check a Merkle signature?

options.text: Verify OTS signature AND recompute root from auth path

questions.explanation: Verification has two steps: (1) verify the OTS signature is valid for the message, and (2) use the authentication path to recompute the root and check it matches the public key.

questions.question: A Merkle signature includes the OTS public key along with the OTS signature.
questions.explanation: True! The signature must include the OTS public key (or its hash) so the verifier can: (1) verify the OTS signature against it, and (2) use it as the starting point for the authentication path verification.

questions.question: The Merkle tree must be fully computed and stored during key generation.
questions.explanation: False! While the root must be computed, efficient traversal algorithms can compute tree nodes on-demand during signing, avoiding the need to store the entire tree. This is important for large trees.
